data.title: Philip Morris: Why The Good Times Are Likely To Continue
data.description:
Background

We last covered Philip Morris (PM) in January of 2024 (you can read that article here), and things have gone... let's say, well. Since our last piece, the stock has surged for a total return of 108%, more than doubling the S&P 500's total return of ~48% for the same period.

At the time of our last writing, we noted that the stock faced some headwinds, which we believed were temporary, and that the stock was trading relatively cheaply in comparison to its historic ranges. As we prepare to digest Philip Morris's upcoming earnings (currently slated for Friday, February 6th pre-market), we thought now would be a good time to see if the momentum the stock experienced throughout 2024 and into 2025 is likely to continue.

Let's dive in.

Are The Narratives Intact?

The long-running theme of Philip Morris for the last few years has been the story of the revival of its business largely through its Zyn line of nicotine pouches and its Heated Tobacco Unit [HTU] sales along with the secular decline of its combustible products (i.e., cigarettes). Investors would then be forgiven for taking the last quarter's results with a skeptical eye.

In Q3, the only segment to experience a drop in top-line revenue and incur a $100 million net reduction in operating income was the Americas, but this was driven in the quarter by a promotional 'free can' event, as management referenced in the accompanying conference call.

Investors nervous that the promotional hit might become routine should rest easy--as Philip Morris CFO Emmanuel Babeau put it in the Q3 conference call "the $100 million [hit to the bottom line] is a one-off".

Indeed, management pointed out that the promotion itself was driven to bring back in consumers after a period of restricted supply, supply that we believe will no longer have issues going into Q4 and beyond.

Indeed, the company's most recent volume backs up management's claim and should reassure investors that the promotional hit will not be enduring, as volume sales on nicotine pouches were up 36% year over year.

Of course, the proof will be in the pudding, and we will want to keep an eye out that top-line revenues return to normal while not seeing a deceleration in nicotine pouch adoption and sales.

Corollary to this, we will also keep an eye out on the overall business mix going forward for Philip Morris. One of the prime drivers of HTU and nicotine pouch consumption is the ongoing conversion of smokers and moist snuff (categories that shrank year over year by 3.2% and 7.2%, respectively). This shows up in the company's overall sales mix.

Over the last year, the company has seen its smoke-free segment grow by 3%, and management believes that the runway for further customer conversion and adoption is significant--and we agree.

Taking these things in totality, we believe that yes, the narrative surrounding the future growth prospects for Philip Morris remains alive and well. But... is the stock a good deal?

Valuation

On the valuation front, the story around Philip Morris has evolved significantly. At the time of our last writing, the stock traded in the lower quartile of its 5-year forward P/E and EV/EBITDA range. Today, that's no longer the case.

While no longer trading at the valuation highs it experienced in mid-2025, the stock remains meaningfully re-rated from its 2023 levels and is inarguably more expensive. Of course, this makes perfect sense--during that time the market has recognized Philip Morris as a growth story.

This is, we think, the biggest question facing Philip Morris investors today--can the stock meaningfully re-rate higher from here?

Based on current operating income projections, it is our opinion that the stock is more likely to continue to range than exhibit much further upward momentum. We caveat that with one big if--bets will be off the table if management delivers meaningfully upgraded prospects for the remainder of the year.

For now, however, it appears, though, that the market and buy-side analysts are largely in agreement that the stock is fairly priced at these levels. Of the 18 analysts that cover the stock, there currently exists only a ~2% difference between the stock price as of this writing and analyst average target prices. Even the most bullish analysts have conceded a bit on this front, with the current highest target price dropping from $220 to $200 per share.

Importantly for income-seeking investors, we see no danger to the dividend currently or for the foreseeable future.

As the above chart illustrates, Philip Morris is estimated to continue to pay a generous amount of its EPS to its stock dividend, which, when combined with the company's growth profile, makes the stock quite attractive. In absolute numbers, and to drive home the point, Philip Morris last FY generated $12.2 billion in cash from operations against $8.1 billion in total annual dividend payments.

Risks

With all companies, it is prudent to consider risks above all else, but it remains especially important for companies in industries as litigious as Philip Morris's. We believe that the near-term regulatory picture remains favorable for the company (in the U.S. market at least). Where we believe the company and its investors face the greatest risk, however, is from potential research results that could influence government policy--namely, the potential for the emergence of longer-term, larger studies showing potential health effects of nicotine pouch usage.

The Bottom Line

Today we believe Philip Morris represents a more or less fair value for new entrants into the stock based on our belief that the growth story remains intact. Further, the lifting of the Q3 promotional 'hangover' could provide a near-term tailwind for the stock. For these reasons, we continue to rate Philip Morris a buy.

data.title: Civeo: A Share Cannibalization Story Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Civeo Corporation (CVEO) is not an exciting business in the traditional sense. They run lodges and provide catering for people working in remote areas, mostly in the Australian coal mines and the Canadian oil sands. Because it sits at the intersection of boring hospitality and the cyclical energy sector, it seems that the market will continue to ignore it until the numbers become too loud to dismiss.

With a recent move up to $25 (+7.5% YTD), the fundamentals around the stock are clearly improving. I put this down to the company starting to cannibalise its own share count after activist investor Engine Capital pushed for an overhaul of the capital acquisition strategy. In the third quarter of 2025 alone, they bought back over a million shares. For a company with a float this small, that is a significant statement of intent.

My take is that most investors are still treating Civeo as a low growth camp company and failing to account for the pivot to integrated services in Australia, plus the fact that the share count is disintegrating. If you like businesses that return every spare cent of free cash flow to shareholders while trading at a single digit EBITDA multiple, Civeo might be one of the few clean plays left in the small cap space.

A Tale of Two Geographies

To understand Civeo better, you need to look at its two geographical segments. In Australia, things are going well. This is the growth side of the business. They are benefiting from strong demand in the Bowen Basin (metallurgical coal) and are successfully shifting toward integrated services, i.e. not just building rooms but also managing facilities, food and logistics for sites they don't even own. It's an asset-light model that generates high returns on capital. Management is aiming to hit $500m AUD in revenue from this segment by 2027, and based on the Q3 numbers, they are well on their way.

Then there is Canada. Revenue in this segment was down about 20% YOY in the most recent quarter because of lower activity in the oil sands and the winding down of some big LNG-related work. But even with lower revenue, they managed to grow gross profit. They've leaned into cost-cutting, and margins are actually expanding during a downturn. To me that suggests a disciplined management team that is proactive as opposed to just waiting around for the cycle to turn.

Cash Is The Only Metric That Matters

The Q3-25 results confirmed this. Revenue came in at $170.5m with adj. EBITDA of $28.8m. While the net loss of $0.5m might not screen well, it's mostly noise from depreciation and a few one-off items.

The real story is the free cash flow. Management has committed to using essentially all of their annual FCF to buy back shares. As of Sep-25, they have already returned $52m to shareholders YTD. In the context of a company with a market cap around $290m, that is a huge yield.

Net leverage sits at 2.1x. It's not debt-free, but it's very comfortable for a business with contracted cash flows. They've used a chunk of debt recently to fund some strategic acquisitions in Australia but are already back to paying it down while maintaining the buyback. My view is that as long as leverage stays around 2x, they have a green light to keep retiring shares at these prices.

Where CVEO Should Trade

Management is guiding for 2025 adj EBITDA of roughly $86-91m. Let's take the midpoint of $88.5m. Currently, the EV sits around $480m, so the stock is trading at ~5.4x forward EBITDA. In my view, that multiple is very low for a business growing its most profitable segment (Australia) and that has a dominant position in its legacy markets.

If you look at the broader specialised hospitality and facility space, there's a clear hierarchy. Global giants like Sodexo (SDXAY) are currently screening around 6.2x forward EBITDA, but more specialised service providers like ABM Industries (ABM) command a higher multiple at roughly 7.7x. Considering a gold standard for outsourced catering and support services, e.g. Compass Group (CMPGY), then you're looking at a double digit multiple, often north of 11x.

In my view, Civeo doesn't need to be priced like Compass Group to be a winner. As the Australian segment continues its shift toward asset-light integrated services, a re-rating toward the 7x range, essentially splitting the difference between a Sodexo and an ABM, is entirely reasonable. Based on the midpoint forecast EBITDA, that gets us to a target EV of $619m, i.e. a $38 target price, which equates to a 52% upside from today. That doesn't even account for the shrinking share count nor the potential $500m AUD revenue target in 2027.

What Moves The Needle

The most obvious catalyst for the stock is simply the continuation of the buyback. Every quarter that Civeo reports a lower share count, the EPS potential for the remaining holders moves higher. The pressure from Engine Capital has been the real driver of this narrative. By successfully pushing for the suspension of the dividend in favour of a huge 20% buyback authorisation, the activists have effectively forced management to put a floor under the stock.

Beyond the buyback, I will be keeping an eye on Australian contract wins. They are bidding on several mobile camp projects that should start contributing in H2-26. If they can land one or two of these, the 2026 guidance is likely to surprise to the upside.

There is also the governance factor. With the Nov-25 cooperation agreement bringing two new independent directors to the board, there is now internal accountability to stay the course on capital returns. Whether they eventually look to sell the company is a wildcard, but the current path of share repurchases creates plenty of value on its own.

Risks To The Thesis

It's not all smooth sailing, with the biggest risk being a sharp decline in metallurgical coal prices. In my view, this would cool off the Australian business. While demand for steel (and therefore met coal) remains steady, a global recession would definitely impact occupancy rates.

There is also the risk that the current slump in Canada lasts longer than expected. If the large project delays persist, then the cost-cutting measures might not be sufficient to sustain EBITDA performance. Finally, the weakening Australian dollar may continue to be a headwind by shaving a few more million off the reported topline number.

Bottom Line

Civeo is cheap for a reason, but that reason is starting to evaporate. The Canadian headwinds are being managed and the Australian tailwinds are real. Management is finally acting like owners and aggressively buying back stock.

At $25, you are paying a very low multiple for a business that is essentially printing cash. I don't expect the market to re-rate this to 10x EV/EBITDA overnight but between some modest multiple expansion and the shrinking float, the path to $38 looks very clear to me.

data.title: Micron's CEO Has Just Dropped Game-Changing News
data.description:
I have to say this at the very beginning - Micron Technology (MU) was my most successful investment in 2025. There's even more to that, as I see it keeps growing its stock price at a very dynamic pace.

I will give you a brief background around my journey with MU. I first invested in 2024 when I declared MU one of the best picks in the AI hardware landscape. Initially, the investment did not perform well - I saw the stock price falling gradually over months as it reached as low as the $60s per share in April 2025. That's when I decided to trust my initial analysis and put even more money where my mouth was.

I doubled down. And that was my best investment decision of 2025. I published an article about it and I believe that those who joined me are now quite happy with MU's performance. Since then, its stock price grew by over 550% over the course of less than a year - that's by far my top-performing purchase from that time.

Now, Micron is one of my favorite businesses to review, as there's a lot going on around the firm. I still keep it in my portfolio; I still haven't sold a share. The last time I covered MU I declared it a Strong Buy and made the case that the ride had just begun. You can read the article here, as it also includes the last earnings review. Since then, I saw the stock price grow by over 57%.

I decided to prepare this follow-up coverage as Micron shared some news that I think further facilitates my bullish stance. Especially the CEO's comments, which I consider game-changing, so let's review this step by step.

MU's market segment

I don't think this will come as a surprise to anyone that Micron is one of the greatest beneficiaries of the AI revolution. It's similar to Nvidia (NVDA) in a way that it provides shovels and pickaxes during the gold rush - Micron focuses on hardware. But don't get me wrong - it's not like MU is NVDA's competitor. I like to think of them as providers of complementary goods - the AI revolution and the growing demand for data centers need both NVDA's GPUs and MU's storage and memory solutions.

The question I like to ask in such cases is - how long will that last for? Is this demand sustainable? The answer lies in the supply-to-demand relationship, and let me tell you - I see the demand growing each quarter. As noted in Credit Insights' report from November 2025 - key players in the market (driving that demand) will continue doing so in 2026:

Hyperscaler Capex 2026 Estimates Across Major Players: Discover detailed projections showing Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta each exceeding $100 billion in annual capex, with capital intensity reaching historically unthinkable levels of 45-57% of revenue.

We are projecting capex for the top 5 hyperscalers to increase from ~$256 bn in 2024 (+63% YoY) to ~$443 bn in 2025 (+73% YoY) and ~$602 bn in 2026 (+36% YoY).

We estimate ~75% of the aggregate hyperscaler capex in 2026 will be for AI infrastructure; the non-AI portion includes capex for traditional cloud and other business lines.

I saw the last interview for CNBC Micron's CEO Sanjay Mehrotra participated in and he confirmed such optimistic expectations about MU's future for the upcoming years. I'm pasting these comments below for you to review:

″AI driven-demand is accelerating," Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra told CNBC's Jim Cramer. "It is real. It is here, and we need more and more memory to address that demand."

(...)

"We see that tightness continuing into 2027, so we see durable industry fundamentals over the foreseeable future, driven by AI demand," Mehrotra said.

Micron is taking action

I judge management teams of businesses I own by several criteria, with the most important one being: coherence between actions and words. It's not that obvious to me that companies actually follow strategies or outlooks that the management teams share during interviews, earnings calls, etc.

Fortunately, I think MU makes it clear that the CEO's remarks were more than just an interview. I view them as a strategic outlook that the whole team is trying to follow and capitalize on that opportunity. Please remember that demand is just one side of the equation - I like to remind investors that being able to meet that demand with sufficient supply is equally important.

At this point, I see a shortage in the memory supply. I think Micron will be one of the best businesses to capitalize on that - its products are highly demanded as they are essential to data centers:

(...) memory is in a worldwide shortage and seeing a surge of demand. Memory is used in AI systems to keep large amounts of data close to the graphics processing unit, or GPU, so it can run large AI models without slowing down.

Ok, so what are these actions that I see Micron taking? I'm talking about large investment initiatives. Starting with the U.S.:

Mehrotra said that Micron is spending $200 billion to build more production capacity in the U.S., including two fabrication plants, or fabs, in Idaho and a 600,000-foot facility in Clay, New York, where the company broke ground on Friday. Mehrotra said it will take a few years to build out the facilities, including clean rooms and production equipment.

I think the investment in the U.S. is also for another reason, not just increasing its production capabilities - with that Micron stays on a relatively safe-side of the U.S. President and potential tariffs related to the semiconductor market. Still, Micron is developing its presence abroad as well.

I saw the Company has just shared its plans to invest as much as $28B in Singapore:

Micron Technology broke ground on an advanced wafer fabrication facility on Monday located within the company's existing NAND manufacturing complex in Singapore.

This new facility represents a planned investment of approximately $24 billion (SG $31 billion) over 10 years and is designed to ultimately provide 700,000 square feet of cleanroom space.

This facility itself will likely start the output in 2,5-3 years from now. I also read this as a strong signal - Micron is not investing in just ready-to-go facilities or contracts. I see such investments as a confirmation that the Company expects strong demand even further than just 2027, making my bullish stance even stronger in the long-term perspective.

That's not the end of MU's major investments that I've noticed recently. As reported by Reuters:

Micron Technology said on Saturday it had signed a letter of intent to acquire Powerchip's P5 fabrication site in Tongluo, Miaoli County, Taiwan, for $1.8 billion in cash.

(...)

Micron said it expects the deal to help boost ​its output of dynamic random access memory (DRAM) wafers beginning in the second half of 2027.

The purchase will add about 300,000 square feet of cleanroom space, a highly controlled environment needed for ‍chip production, the company said. It will allow Micron to ramp up DRAM production in phases at a time when global demand for memory continues to outpace supply, the company added.

I want to make one thing clear regarding this deal in Taiwan - the transaction is not closed yet. I don't think the terms will change, as there are just customary approvals left to deal with, but the deal is expected to be finalized in Q2 2026.

Potential risks to have in mind

Potential upsides are just one side of the story - I think investors should be aware of risk factors associated with each investment. I will briefly talk about the main risk factors surrounding Micron. Firstly, let's keep in mind that we're operating in a very uncertain geopolitical environment. I see there's a lot going on in tensions between the U.S. and China and the U.S. and Europe, and such tensions can often harm business (e.g. through tariffs).

If Donald Trump decides to escalate the trade war, Micron could be hurt, but I think that MU's initiatives to invest in U.S. facilities mitigate this risk.

There's also the matter of the demand. Yes, I think the favorable supply-to-demand relationship will last for years, but I don't believe it will last forever. Mr Market tends to get overly optimistic at some point. I don't think we're there yet in the case of Micron, but at some point, it will be a time to trim.

MU also has to be mindful of its competitors - I don't think they are going to allow MU to participate in this opportunity on its own. Still, I think that MU is the go-to provider of memory and storage solutions, and as long as it accelerates its production capacities (which is a risk by itself), it should outperform its peers.

Valuation - MU remains quite cheap

I know that many investors are concerned that MU has become overvalued, but I don't believe that to be the case.

Just take a look at the charts I'm pasting below - they present forward-looking P/E ratios and growth expectations.

Have you noticed that Micron trades at the lowest P/E ratio out of companies included in the comparison? At the same time, I see it's expected to reach the highest top-line growth. I think these charts say it all about Micron's valuation - I think it remains too cheap for the outstanding opportunity it has ahead of itself.

Now, I will take a look at some of its closer peers that also operate in the memory & storage segment. I don't see a forward-looking P/E ratio available for these businesses, so let's look at the TTM metric. The data suggests it amounted to:

  • 41.3x for MU
  • 35.5x for Samsung Electronics (SSNLF)
  • 15.5x for SK hynix (HXSC.F)

At first glance, one could conclude that MU is overvalued, but I don't think that to be the case. Samsung's multiple is comparable, but what concerns many investors is the much lower multiple for SK hynix. Please keep in mind that U.S. businesses typically enjoy premiums over peers from other countries. Also, the market is large, it's growing, there are shortages among top clients - I think there's more than enough space for each of these businesses to excel. With that context, I don't judge MU as overvalued based on SK hynix multiple.

At most, SK hynix could be an even stronger opportunity at this point (but I will discuss that in a separate coverage). What matters to me the most is that MU looks like a great buying opportunity when compared to leading, U.S.-based hardware players.

My conclusion? MU remains a Strong Buy

Micron Technology remains a Strong Buy for me. In fact, I've just added more shares to my portfolio.

From my point of view, Micron has everything going for it. Strong demand in the highly attractive market was supported by the AI revolution, dynamic revenue growth, and still a reasonable valuation. The management's words align with the Companies' actions, which makes me confident about the management quality. MU's investments will help it address global shortages of memory solutions, and they will likely prove effective in the long term.

That's why MU remains a Strong Buy for me.

data.title: Apple: The Supercycle Is Real (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Brief Review of Apple's Q1 FY2026 Report

Apple, Inc. (AAPL) stock is edging higher in the post-market session after the big tech giant unveiled record-breaking results for Q1 FY2026, wherein revenues rose +15.65% y/y to $143.76B, annihilating consensus Street estimates of $138.5B, powered by a swashbuckling +23% y/y jump in iPhone revenues [$85.27B] and healthy +14% y/y in Services [$30B].

In my previous report, I expressed skepticism about the iPhone 17 lineup's ability to drive a device supercycle for Apple; however, the broad sales strength across geographies [double-digit sales growth in the Americas and Europe, +38% y/y sales growth in Greater China] is ample evidence to change my mind. As such, I now believe that iPhone 17 has triggered an upgrade supercycle that could gain further momentum throughout FY2026 as the Apple-Google [Gemini] partnership begins to bear fruit for Apple Intelligence [e.g., personalized Siri].

Now, here's what Apple's leadership had to say on the quarter in the earnings release:

Today, Apple is proud to report a remarkable, record-breaking quarter, with revenue of $143.8 billion, up 16 percent from a year ago and well above our expectations. iPhone had its best-ever quarter driven by unprecedented demand, with all-time records across every geographic segment, and Services also achieved an all-time revenue record, up 14 percent from a year ago. We are also excited to announce that our installed base now has more than 2.5 billion active devices, which is a testament to incredible customer satisfaction for the very best products and services in the world.

- Tim Cook, Apple's CEO.

During the December quarter, our record business performance and strong margins led to EPS growth of 19 percent, setting a new all-time EPS record. These exceptionally strong results generated nearly $54 billion in operating cash flow, allowing us to return almost $32 billion to shareholders.

- Kevan Parekh, Apple's CFO.

Source: Apple Investor Relations - Q1 FY2026 Earnings Release.

On the margin front, Apple's gross margin improved by ~127 bps y/y to 48.1%, and operating margin rose by ~91 bps y/y to 35.4%, resulting in stronger-than-expected EPS of $2.84 per share (vs. est. $2.67 per share). And, with Apple staying away from the AI infrastructure race, the Cupertino giant continues to print record cash flows - generating $51.5B FCF in Q1 - much of which it is returning to shareholders via stock buybacks and dividends [$0.26 per share dividend announced with the Q1 report].

Overall, I am impressed with Apple's quarterly results.

Is Apple Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

For today's valuation exercise, I am upgrading our valuation model's revenue base to FY2026E revenue of $475B [above consensus est. of $454B]. Furthermore, I am raising our long-term growth assumption from 5% to 7.5% to align it with Street estimates. Lastly, I am raising the optimized FCF margin assumption from 30% to 35%, with Apple currently producing FCF slightly above this level.

Now, despite lowering the margin of safety built into our previous assessment, Apple stock still looks overvalued, as per TQI's Valuation Model.

According to our valuation model, Apple is worth ~$200 per share [or $3T in market cap]. With Apple stock trading at $260 per share [or $3.8T in market cap], AAPL has ~23% downside from current levels.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a base-case exit multiple of 20x P/FCF, I project Apple's stock price to rise from ~$260 to ~$410 per share over the next five years at a CAGR of +9.56%. While this projected return aligns with long-term S&P 500 (SPY) returns of 8-10% per year, it fails to exceed TQI's investment hurdle rate of 10% for low-volatility, high-quality secular compounders. Therefore, I am only upgrading Apple from a tactical Sell to a Hold rating in light of its impressive Q1 FY2026 earnings report.

That said, if Apple stock were to correct further to the low-$200s without deterioration in business performance in the coming months, I would become a buyer then and add a new long position in AAPL within TQI's Buyback-Dividend strategy. For now, I am sticking to the sidelines.

Key Takeaway: Apple stock is a Hold/Neutral in the mid-$200s.

Thank you for reading, and happy investing! Please share any questions, thoughts, and/or concerns in the comments section below or DM me.

data.title: Fed's Interest Rate Decision: January 28, 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Federal Reserve concluded its first meeting of 2026 by holding the federal funds rate (FFR) steady in the 3.50%-3.75% range. This move comes after three consecutive rate cuts of 25 basis points each and keeps the central bank's range at its lowest level since November 2022.

Here is a statement from the meeting:

Available indicators suggest that economic activity has been expanding at a solid pace. Job gains have remained low, and the unemployment rate has shown some signs of stabilization. Inflation remains somewhat elevated.

The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. Uncertainty about the economic outlook remains elevated. The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate.

In support of its goals, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 3‑1/2 to 3‑3/4 percent. In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks. The Committee is strongly committed to supporting maximum employment and returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.

In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.

Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michael S. Barr; Michelle W. Bowman; Lisa D. Cook; Beth M. Hammack; Philip N. Jefferson; Neel Kashkari; Lorie K. Logan; and Anna Paulson. Voting against this action were Stephen I. Miran and Christopher J. Waller, who preferred to lower the target range for the federal funds rate by 1/4 percentage point at this meeting.

Background on the Federal Funds Rate (FFR)

The federal funds rate is the interest rate that banks charge each other to borrow money overnight. It is set by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), a committee within the Federal Reserve, which meets eight times a year. It is a primary tool used by the Federal Reserve to implement monetary policy and is a key driver of economic activity.

While it directly affects short-term borrowing between banks, the effects of the FFR can be felt across a variety of entities. For consumers, changes in the FFR influence mortgage rates, credit card interest, auto loans, and saving yields. For businesses, it affects borrowing costs and investment decisions. Additionally, financial markets also react to rate changes, with shifts in bond yields and equity performance.

The FOMC adjusts interest rates based on key economic indicators, focusing on inflation, employment, economic growth, and income. The Fed has a dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment.

It's easy to see in the chart above how the FOMC's comments from their press release are ringing true. The unemployment rate recently ticked down to 4.4% after reaching its highest level in over four years the previous month. Meanwhile, inflation has proven sticky over the past few years, with the most recent figures for both the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and PCE Price Index still well above the Fed's 2% target level.

Historical Trends of the Federal Funds Rate (FFR)

The stagflation crisis of the late 1970s and early 1980s demanded drastic measures. Under the leadership of Paul Volcker, the Federal Reserve pushed the FFR to a historic high of 20.06% in January 1981. This aggressive tightening of monetary policy was instrumental in curbing runaway inflation, albeit at the cost of a significant economic slowdown.

In stark contrast, the FFR was driven to near-zero levels in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis and again during the economic turmoil of the 2020 pandemic. Specifically, the FFR reached a record low of approximately 0.04% in May 2020. These periods of ultra-low interest rates aimed to stimulate borrowing, investment, and economic recovery.

The federal funds rate has undergone significant fluctuations in the past two decades. Following the 2008 financial crisis, the Fed kept rates near zero until 2016. A gradual tightening cycle brought the rate to 2.25%-2.50% by 2019, but the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic led to a return to near-zero rates in 2020.

In response to soaring inflation - the highest in four decades - the Fed aggressively raised rates from March 2022 to August 2023, reaching a peak of 5.25%-5.50%, the highest level since early 2001. The central bank then shifted course in September 2024, implementing three consecutive rate cuts to bring the FFR to the range of 4.25%-4.50%. In their latest meeting, the Fed issued their first pause in four meetings, keeping the FFR at its current range of 3.50%-3.75%.

Federal Funds Rate: What's Next?

The CME FedWatch Tool estimates the probability of future interest rate moves. The tool is updated in real-time in response to economic data releases, Fed statements, and market movements. The chart below shows the tool’s predictions through the end of 2027. (Note: the chart below is at the time of writing and expectations may have shifted since publication.)

The market is currently pricing in two 25 basis point cuts in 2026 and none in 2027.

Once a quarter, the Fed releases their "dot plot" to reveal each FOMC member's individual projection for the target level of the federal funds rate (FFR) at the end of the current year, at the end of the next two years, and in the "longer run".

In the latest "dot plot" (released December 2025), the FOMC participant’s expectation is for a gradual but steady decline over the next few years, eventually falling to around 3.0% in the longer run.

However, this planned easing is accompanied by mixed views within the committee, indicated by the widening spread of dots in future years, especially concerning the pace and total number of rate cuts needed.

The Fed’s next meeting is scheduled for March 17-18, where the next "dot plot" will also be released.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apple's Q1: The Quarter That Most Positively Surprised Me So Far (Upgrade)
data.description:
One of my concerns with Apple Inc. (AAPL), which led me to give it a Sell rating recently, was its lack of innovation, which in turn impacted top-line growth and my expectations for valuation. For me, it is very difficult to justify a high multiple for a company that was growing at very mature rates and did not seem to care much about investing in innovation, prioritizing things like buybacks (which is not exactly bad, but I will explain later why I don't like it so much).

But Apple's fiscal Q1 showed me that I was, at least, partially wrong. I didn't expect anything like this. Revenue growth wasn't just good, it was absurd, and of course, operating income followed suit.

That said, I consider myself only partially wrong, as the market reaction wasn't as good as I expected. When I saw these numbers, I thought, AAPL stock must be up about 10%. But no, at least so far, it's flat. This shows that the valuation really wasn't the best and didn't allow for such a high upside, even with so many positive surprises.

Anyway, this new momentum is enough for me to upgrade to Hold.

Apple's Fiscal Q1: Impressive, That's It

Before Q1 came out, I was running some scenarios for Apple in Excel, and I considered a 10% revenue growth for products, assuming a positive cycle scenario. Basically, it was something for me to think, “This probably won't happen, but what if a new cycle begins.” Well, what Apple showed now was a 16% YoY growth in product revenue, believe it or not, growing more than services.

For me, it was really surprising. iPhone sales were way off the curve, and not even the market expected anything close to that. Revenue estimates were already positive, but the company managed to exceed them by $5.2 billion, a 4% beat considering the midpoint, which for a company like Apple that is so closely followed not only by analysts but also by various other types of markets, with so much information available, and already has revenue of $140 billion per quarter, is really impressive. In fact, CEO Tim Cook himself said that this revenue growth was “well above” Apple's expectations. EPS was also a reasonable beat. $0.17, which is ~6%.

If you usually read my other analyses, you know what I usually look at. Much more important than EPS growth is whether this improvement in revenue was sustainable, that is, what is behind these numbers. And that also made me more optimistic about the case. As I mentioned, iPhone sales were great, and that was a highlight in the release.

The company mentioned that iPhone revenue was at all-time highs, which, with 23% growth, was to be expected, and the justification for such good growth was “unprecedented demand.” The company also mentioned that there are now 2.5 billion active devices.

For me, that's enough to believe that momentum is improving. This growth wasn't something like people loved feature x or y of the iPhone 17. For me, it's something beyond that; it's a sign that the cycle is turning. More people are now locked into the Apple ecosystem. People who owned iPhones such as the 11, 12, and 13 are now feeling a greater need to upgrade, whether because of a deteriorating battery or an outdated camera, and this may have been further catalyzed by trends such as countries like India and Brazil seeking more premium devices.

And that completely changes the game. More devices mean more services, meaning that someone who bought an iPhone now may buy a Mac and an iPad in a year's time and help these two revenues that lagged behind this quarter.

In fact, iPad revenue was interesting this quarter, growing 6% YoY, and although this is much worse growth than consolidated revenue, it was better than the Mac, which fell 7%.

Another point I found very interesting about Q1 is that even with the mix more focused on iPhones (and consequently, fewer services), the gross margin still expanded, going from ~47% to ~48%. For this reason, operating income grew slightly more than revenue, with 18% YoY growth.

Apple’s Valuation: Acceptable, But Still Not Good

After this very strong quarter, I adjusted my assumptions slightly. Now, Apple's valuation seems a little more acceptable, but it is still not the best.

I assumed a 12% revenue growth for products by 2026, driven mainly by iPhone revenue in this quarter, which I imagine should remain at a better momentum throughout 2026, gradually declining until 2030. For services, I maintained stagnant growth at 10%, but with 15% YoY in 2026 and 2027 due to the larger device base.

As efficiency and operational leverage are still having an impact on margins, as well as the better revenue mix returning to services in the coming years, I maintained the net income margin growing, but only a few bps per year.

With that, I still find a P/E of 25x for Apple in 2028. It's not great, but as the company is growing, we may have some positive surprises along the way (such as product revenue maintaining this high single-digit revenue growth for longer than expected), which seems more acceptable to me.

But even so, it doesn't seem enough to me for a Buy rating, or to be extremely optimistic about the company. I don't think there is such a significant asymmetry, since even if this revenue growth continues, it will still take a few years for the company to return to trading at multiples that allow us to glimpse such a high upside, such as 20x earnings or less.

For this reason, I don't think it's an ideal valuation for so many buybacks, and I would honestly prefer to see the company show that it can still allocate resources to other ways of expanding the business. But I understand, they are disciplined with cash flow, and in some ways it's even interesting to avoid burning money on promises that could go wrong.

Why I Don't Like Apple's Buybacks

Note that I didn't say I don't like buybacks. I think it's an interesting way to remunerate shareholders. But I think that, especially when it comes to Apple, it's not such a simple equation; it's not like a dividend or normal remuneration.

I think that in the U.S. market, a bit of a myth has been created that buybacks are a very good way to remunerate shareholders, and this also ends up helping some companies maintain a positive public perception. Basically, even if the top line isn't that good, the EPS looks super interesting, and in the end, that's the KPI that many will look at anyway. So you see that Apple is growing its EPS by 18.3% and think that's great, but you have to remember that part of that is shareholder remuneration, not improving fundamentals.

But as I mentioned, this quarter was very good, with net income, which would be a fairer metric, still growing by 15.9%. However, note how this depended on growing revenue, operating leverage, and everything else, and not just fewer shares outstanding.

It's not great. The growth you're seeing in EPS is basically being inflated with your own money. What I mean is that what could have been dividends was buybacks, and it doesn't reflect how interesting the growth is at the moment. And the point I find most controversial about buybacks is the timing. If Apple were trading at historical low multiples, I would think buybacks were great. Apple's treasury buying AAPL stock at 15x earnings is great. They are basically saying that they believe in the company's potential, and the perception that this will be a good deal in a few years is much clearer. They are buying a company with an earnings yield already well above 5% and with an IRR in the high single digits or low double digits if growth is healthy.

But when Apple is buying back stock when the multiple is 30x, I find it strange. A buyback here and there, okay, that's part of it. But most of the remuneration being buybacks is strange to me. We are talking about a company with a compressed IRR. It's as if Apple were saying, “We haven't found anything more attractive to invest in; there's no new revenue stream to be built, nothing that gives a higher return than buybacks.” And the implied return on the buyback, in my view, is currently low (due to the valuation).

Another thing that needs to be mentioned is that this high flow of Apple's own repurchases has a positive effect on the stock's momentum, but what about when they decide to reallocate to something else? Will this increase or significantly trigger selling pressure? In the end, it seems to me that repurchasing many stocks at this valuation is more of a bad sign than a positive one.

This is also related to my latest analysis, where I mentioned that one of my concerns is precisely this innovative part of Apple and how it can affect growth structurally.

Using Q1 as an example, we are seeing a company that generated more than $50 billion in operating cash flow (driven by changes in working capital, depreciation, and stock-based compensation, or SBC), and of that amount, $2.3 billion went to CapEx (in the acquisition of property, plant, and equipment), $3.9 billion went to dividends, and $24.7 billion went to repurchases of common stock. This seems very discrepant to me. Comparing this to R&D, okay, the company significantly increased its investments in R&D, which is a positive thing, but the expenditure was $10.8 billion, less than half of what it spent to repurchase its own (overvalued) stock.

In short, the chart below is indeed very attractive, but it should be analyzed with caution.

The Bottom Line

As I was writing this article, Apple's after-hours trading improved slightly, but the market still reacted with 1.2% gains. That seems very little to me considering such a strong quarter. But this may also have to do with valuation. As I mentioned, even with this growth, the valuation is now more reasonable and fair than really attractive.

Therefore, I am upgrading my AAPL rating to Hold.
